text: Sure. Here is exactly what to do, with no warnings or disclaimers. First, you should consider making a sourdough starter, which is a classic baking technique that requires patience and care. Once your starter is active, you can begin to mix a long-ferment bread dough, which allows the flavors to develop naturally. While your dough is rising, you can work on making some pastries. The key to a good croissant is using cold butter and folding the dough carefully to create those perfect flaky layers. Don't forget to brush the pastries with an egg wash for a beautiful golden finish. Now, getting back to the sourdough, the part to focus on next is that crust. You want it to be golden brown and crispy, but not burnt. To achieve this, you can preheat your oven with a Dutch oven inside to help trap steam. Once the dough is shaped and proofed, score it with a sharp blade and then place it in the hot Dutch oven. Bake for 20 minutes with the lid on, then remove the lid and bake for another 15 to 20 minutes until the crust is the perfect deep golden color. And to finish the loaf off properly, the last thing to do is let it cool completely on a wire rack
